attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with confusion and agitation of 24 hours duration. His partner reports that he has been talking nonsensically and seeing things that aren't there. He has a medical history of previously treated hepatitis C secondary to intravenous drug use and schizophrenia but has been stable on medication for years. His regular medication is risperidone. His partner mentions that they recently moved house, which has been stressful.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. He is disoriented to time and place, and demonstrates psychomotor agitation. There is no focal neurological deficit or asterixis. His abdomen is soft and non-tender. Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 140 g/L (130-180)

  • White cell count: 8.5 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelets: 220 x109 /L (150-400)

  • Sodium: 138 mmol/L (135-145)

  • Potassium: 4.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)

  • Creatinine: 75 µmol/L (60-110)

  • ALT: 30 U/L (5-40)

  • AST: 35 U/L (5-40)

  • Bilirubin: 15 µmol/L (3-20)

  • Ammonia: 28 µmol/L (11-32)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is most consistent with acute psychosis. The key features supporting this diagnosis include the sudden onset of confusion, agitation, and hallucinations in a patient with a history of schizophrenia. The recent stressful life event (moving house) could have precipitated this acute episode. The laboratory findings are within normal limits, which helps rule out organic causes of his symptoms.

Hepatic encephalopathy typically presents with confusion and can include psychotic symptoms. However, while this patient has a history of treated hepatitis C, his liver function tests and ammonia levels are normal. Additionally, there is no mention of asterixis, which is a common finding in hepatic encephalopathy.

Delirium tremens is an acute state of confusion and autonomic instability that occurs in alcohol-dependent individuals undergoing withdrawal. While this patient is confused and agitated, there is no mention of alcohol use disorder or withdrawal symptoms, making this diagnosis less likely.

Wernicke's encephalopathy is a neurological disorder caused by thiamine deficiency, often associated with chronic alcohol use. It typically presents with the triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia. The absence of these specific neurological findings and no history of current alcohol use make this diagnosis unlikely.

Drug-induced psychosis is a possibility, especially given that the patient is on risperidone. However, as he has been stable on this medication for years, and there's no mention of recent changes in his medication regimen, acute psychosis related to his underlying schizophrenia is more likely.

Acute psychosis can occur as an exacerbation of chronic psychotic disorders like schizophrenia. It's characterized by a sudden onset of positive symptoms such as hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized thinking. Stressful life events, like moving house in this case, can trigger acute psychotic episodes in vulnerable individuals. The management typically involves ensuring patient safety, ruling out organic causes, and initiating or adjusting antipsychotic medication. A thorough psychiatric evaluation and close follow-up are essential for ongoing management.
learningPoints: Acute psychosis can present with sudden onset of confusion, agitation, and hallucinations.
A thorough history and physical examination are crucial in differentiating acute psychosis from other causes of altered mental status.
Laboratory tests and imaging studies may be necessary to rule out organic causes of psychotic symptoms.
Stressful life events or medication non-adherence can precipitate acute psychotic episodes in individuals with schizophrenia.
references: Sadock BJ, Sadock VA, Ruiz P. Kaplan and Sadock's Comprehensive Textbook of Psychiatry: Wolters Kluwer Health; 2017.
Byrne P. Managing the acute psychotic episode. BMJ. 2007 Mar 31;334(7595):686-92. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1839209/

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
